coredata.dc:title: A ratiometric luminescence probe based on lanthanide metal–organic framework@polyethyleneimine for sensitive and selective detection of Cu2+ and Fe3+
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Kexin
Han, Zhenglin
Wen, Xiaokun
Fan, Xiaoxuan
Yin, Feifei
Guan, Baijie
hong, Xia
Wang, Jiwei
Liu, Wen
Tan, Tianya
coredata.dc:description: The development of probes for the detection of multiple heavy metal ions holds the nonnegligible practical significance for ensuring public and environment safety. In this work, a ratiometric luminescence probe was proposed for the detection Cu2+ and Fe3+ based lanthanide metal–organic framework@polyethyleneimine (Ln-MOF@PEI). The formation of the complexes between PEI and Cu2+ enhances the quenching efficiency of MOF through a combination of inner filter effect and energy transfer, surpassing the observed quenching efficiency with Fe3+. The emission intensity ratio between Tb3+ and the ligands exhibits a decreasing trend with increasing Cu2+ concentration, while it rises with increasing Fe3+ concentration. It allows for the discrimination between Cu2+ and Fe3+ based on their distinct quenching mechanisms. The assay performance of Ln-MOF@PEI probes was evaluated comprehensively. The limit of detection for Cu2+ and Fe3+ reaches at 9.7 nM and 37.4 nM, respectively. The probe demonstrates acceptable selectivity by effectively avoiding interference from common cations and anions. The detection range covers a wide concentration range from nanomoles to millimoles. Overall, the luminescence probe is expected to meet the requirements of accurate quantification for Cu2+ and Fe3+ in the various application scenarios.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Metal–organic framework
Probe
Cu2+ detection
Fe3+ detection
Rare earth luminescence

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Evaluation of detection sensitivity for Cu2+ and Fe3+
Ratiometric luminescence detection of Cu2+ and Fe3+

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grants No. 51972052 ), the Open Foundation of Key Laboratory for UV-Emitting Materials and Technology of Ministry of Education (Northeast Normal University, No. 135131009), the Doctoral Start-up Foundation and Natural Science Foundation of Liaoning Province (2021-BS-085 and 2021-MS-148), Shenyang Science and Technology Bureau (22-315-6-06) and General Project of the Department of Education of Liaoning Province (LJKZ0084).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) SEM image of Ln-MOF. (b) XRD patterns of Ln-MOFs with various doping of Tb3+ and Y3+. (c) FTIR spectra of Ln-MOF and Ln-MOF@PEI. (d) Zeta potentials of Ln-MOF and Ln-MOF@PEI. (e) Nitrogen adsorption–desorption isotherms of Ln-MOF. (f) Nitrogen adsorption–desorption isotherms of Ln-MOF@PEI.
(a) Emission spectra of Ln-MOF with various doping of Tb3+ and Y3+ under 297 nm excitation. (b) Energy level diagram of Tb3+ under 297 nm excitation.
Emission spectra of Ln-MOF@PEI dispersion with various pH values (3–12).

(a) Normalized absorption spectra of Ln-MOF@PEI-Cu2+ complex (black curve) and emission spectrum of Ln-MOF@PEI-Cu2+ complex (blue curve). (b) Luminescence lifetimes (546 nm) of Ln-MOF, Ln-MOF@PEI and Ln-MOF@PEI-Cu2+ complex under 297 nm excitation. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(a) Emission spectra of the Ln-MOF@PEI dispersion upon the addition of the various concentrations of Cu2+. (b) Linear relationship between I546 nm/I402 nm (the ratio of emission intensity at 546 nm and 402 nm) and logarithm of Cu2+. Error bars indicate standard deviations from the mean of 5 parallel results. (c) Emission spectra of the Ln-MOF@PEI dispersion upon the addition of the various concentrations of Fe3+. (d) Linear relationship between I546 nm/I402 nm and logarithm of Fe3+. Error bars indicate standard deviations from the mean of 5 parallel results.

Selectivity of Ln-MOF@PEI probe for Cu2+ and Fe3+ over common metal ions and anions in water. Error bars indicate standard deviations from the mean of 5 parallel results. The concentrations of the interfering ion in the selectivity experiments are all 1 mM. The concentration of Cu2+ and Fe3+ is 1 μM.
Comparison of analytical performance for Cu2+ and Fe3+ detection of the methods in recent years.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: BPEI: branched polyethylenimine; SERS: surface enhanced Raman scattering; AgNPs: Ag nanoparticles; PVP: polyvinylpyrrolidine; Ru-HPIP: Ru(bpy)2/2-(2-hydroxyphenyl)imidazo[4,5-f][1,10]phenanthroline;

head.ce:title: A ratiometric luminescence probe based on lanthanide metal–organic framework@polyethyleneimine for sensitive and selective detection of Cu2+ and Fe3+

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Physics, Liaoning University, Shenyang 110036, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Physics, Liaoning University, Shenyang 110036, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of UV-Emitting Materials and Technology (Northeast Normal University), Ministry of Education, Changchun 130024, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of UV-Emitting Materials and Technology (Northeast Normal University)

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of UV-Emitting Materials and Technology (Northeast Normal University), Ministry of Education, Changchun 130024, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The development of probes for the detection of multiple heavy metal ions holds the nonnegligible practical significance for ensuring public and environment safety. In this work, a ratiometric luminescence probe was proposed for the detection Cu2+ and Fe3+ based lanthanide metal–organic framework@polyethyleneimine (Ln-MOF@PEI). The formation of the complexes between PEI and Cu2+ enhances the quenching efficiency of MOF through a combination of inner filter effect and energy transfer, surpassing the observed quenching efficiency with Fe3+. The emission intensity ratio between Tb3+ and the ligands exhibits a decreasing trend with increasing Cu2+ concentration, while it rises with increasing Fe3+ concentration. It allows for the discrimination between Cu2+ and Fe3+ based on their distinct quenching mechanisms. The assay performance of Ln-MOF@PEI probes was evaluated comprehensively. The limit of detection for Cu2+ and Fe3+ reaches at 9.7 nM and 37.4 nM, respectively. The probe demonstrates acceptable selectivity by effectively avoiding interference from common cations and anions. The detection range covers a wide concentration range from nanomoles to millimoles. Overall, the luminescence probe is expected to meet the requirements of accurate quantification for Cu2+ and Fe3+ in the various application scenarios.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Heavy metal pollution is a serious problem that accompanies the wave of industrialization. It gets accumulated in plants and drinking water, ultimately posing a threat to human life and health through the food chain [1–3]. The excessive intake of heavy metal elements serves as a trigger for multiple diseases, such as Wilson's disease (excessive copper intake) and hepatic fibrosis (excessive iron intake) [4,5]. The detection of heavy metal ions is of indispensable practical significance in public health and environmental monitoring. Current methods for heavy metal ion detection include atomic absorption spectroscopy, atomic emission spectroscopy, inductively coupled plasma mass spectroscopy, and fluorescence spectroscopy [6–9]. Among these methods, fluorescence spectroscopy shows advantages in terms of sensitivity, cost, and simplicity [10,11]. Traditional fluorescence methods rely on the changes in the intensity of the single emission peak for quantitative assay of ions [12–14]. It is susceptible to environmental interferences, leading to potential inaccuracies in detection.

Recently, ratiometric luminescence probes have garnered significant interest among researchers [15–17]. They are considered to possess self-calibration capabilities to ensure the detection accuracy. The two emission bands serve as an intrinsic reference, eliminating most interference factors, such as photobleaching, probe concentration, instrumental efficiency, and environmental conditions [18,19]. However, the ratiometric luminescence technique is still in its early stages of development. Most ratiometric luminescence probes can only specifically detect a single heavy metal ion, making it challenging to meet the increasing and complex detection requirements. It is crucial to further optimize the design of probes to meet the practical application needs of heavy metal ion detection.

The development of novel optical materials provides numerous opportunities for the design of ratiometric fluorescence probes. Lanthanide metal–organic frameworks (Ln-MOFs), which are hybrid materials self-assembled from organic ligands and lanthanide metal ions building units, have found applications in the field of biochemical sensing [10,11,20–22]. The characteristic emission from 4f electronic configuration of lanthanides ions has large Stokes shifts, high color purity and relatively long luminescence lifetimes. The “antenna effect” of the organic framework further enhances the luminescence performance of Ln-MOFs through energy transfer between the organic ligands and lanthanide ions [23,24]. Taking advantages of their exceptional luminescence performance, Ln-MOFs have been developed as luminescence probes and have demonstrated acceptable assay performance in the single heavy metal ion detection [23,25]. But they are still incompetent for the ratio luminescence detection of multiple ions, particularly in terms of detection specificity. Its constraint hampers the practical applications of Ln-MOF probes.

In this work, a ratiometric luminescence probe was proposed based on Ln-MOF@polyethylenimine (PEI). The probe is capable of detecting for Cu2+ and Fe3+ through luminescence quenching. Cu2+ and Fe3+ exhibit different quenching effects on the luminescence of 1,3,5-triethylbenzene (BTC) ligands and Tb3+. PEI on the surface of Ln-MOF has a strong chelation on Cu2+ to form the complexes. The complexes have the inner filter effect and energy transfer between Tb3+. It induced the more effective quenching comparing with Fe3+. The distinct quenching mechanisms allow for the accurate discrimination between Cu2+ and Fe3+ by analyzing the ratio of emission intensities of BTC ligands and Tb3+. The sensitivity, selectivity and mechanisms of the detection for Cu2+ and Fe3+ were studied systematically. These findings would contribute to the development and practicality of the detection technology for multiple heavy metal ions.

Tb2O3, Y2O3 and CH3COONa were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co, Ltd. BTC, N,N-dmethylformamide (DMF) and PEI were purchased from Macklin Reagent Co, Ltd. Methanol, copper chloride (CuCl2), ferric chloride (FeCl3), aluminium chloride (AlCl3), barium chloride (BaCl2), lead chloride (PbCl2), calcium chloride (CaCl2), zinc chloride (ZnCl2), hydrochloric acid (HCI), nitric acid (HNO3), acetic acid (CH3COOH), phosphoric acid (H3PO4) and sulphuric acid (H2SO4) were purchased from Beijing Chemical Co, Ltd. Tb2O3 and Y2O3 were dissolved in HNO3 and evaporated the solution to obtain the powders of Tb(NO3)3 and Y(NO3)3. The purity of all chemical reagents is analytical pure. Deionized water was used in all aqueous solutions.

The Ln-MOF was prepared according to the reported method with necessary modifications [22]. Tb(NO3)3 (0.0093 g), Y(NO3)3 (0.3524 g), and CH3COONa (0.0014 g) were firstly dissolved in deionized water (22.6 mL) by ultrasonic bath. Next the solution was mixed with the BTC DMF solution (0.5 mM, 40 mL) under vigorous stirring for 30 min to obtain a white emulsion. The emulsion was added into a hydrothermal reacted vessel and placed at 60 ℃ for 24 h and cooled down to room temperature. The reaction solution was centrifuged and washed by DMF for 3 times. The white precipitate was collected and then dispersed in methanol (50 mL) to be activated for 24 h. The products were separated by centrifugation and washed by methanol for 3 times. They were dried in the vacuum at 80 ℃ for 12 h to obtain the white powder of Ln-MOF.

The Ln-MOF was dispersed in PEI solution (1 mM, 20 mL) to be surface functionalized by stirring at room temperature for 2 h. The Ln-MOF@PEI were collected by centrifugation and washed by deionized water for 3 times. The powder samples of the Ln-MOF@PEI were obtained by vacuum freeze drying.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation of detection sensitivity for Cu2+ and Fe3+
ce:section.ce:para: A stock CuCl2 and FeCl3 solution were first prepared. A series of CuCl2 and FeCl3 solution with the specific concentration (500 μM, 100 μM, 50 μM, 10 μM, 5 μM, 1 μM, 500 nM, 100 nM, 50 nM and 10 nM) were diluted by the stock solution as the samples to be determined. A volume of 200 μL of Cu2+ or Fe3+ solution with the specific concentrations was added into 1800 μL of Ln-MOF@PEI dispersion in a cuvette at the pH of ∼8. After thorough shaking by hands, the fluorescence emission spectra of the dispersions were collected under the 297 nm excitation.

Interferents including AlCl3, BaCl2, PbCl2, CaCl2, ZnCl2, HCI, HNO3, CH3COOH, H3PO4 and H2SO4 were used to replace CuCl2 or FeCl3 for the verification of the selectivity. All other detection steps were carried out as described in section 2.3, except that the concentrations of the interfering ions were determined to 1 mM.
The real water samples were collected from Xinkai river in Shenyang City. They were detected without further processing. All other detection procedures were carried out as described in Section 2.3.

The morphology of the Ln-MOF was characterized by the scanning electron microscope (SEM, Quanta, FEG-250). X-ray diffraction (XRD) data of Ln-MOF were tested by the Tongda TD-3500 automatic X-ray diffractometer system. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) transmittance spectra of the Ln-MOF and Ln-MOF@PEI were characterized by the Nicolet IS10 spectrometer under the liquid nitrogen environment. The surface potentials of the Ln-MOF and Ln-MOF@PEI were measured by Zeta potentiometric tester (Nano-ZS90, Malvern). The fluorescence emission spectra were recorded on a Hitachi F-7000 fluorescence spectrometer under 297 nm excitation.

The preparation of MOF and doping of Tb3+ and Y3+ were accomplished by a one-pot hydrothermal method. The SEM image of Ln-MOF is shown in Fig. 1 a. The Ln-MOFs exhibit the rod-like shape with the average length of 2.81 ± 0.31 μm and width of 0.51 ± 0.05 μm. The XRD patterns of Ln-MOF are shown in Fig. 1b, showing well-indexed diffraction peaks corresponding to the JUC-32 phase. The positions and widths of diffraction peaks have almost no distinctions with the different mole ratios of Tb3+ and Y3+. It means that the concentration variation of Tb3+ and Y3+ has no impact on the crystal structure, due to their similar coordination numbers.

The chemical composition of the Ln-MOF was further investigated by FTIR spectra (Fig. 1c). The broad band centered at 3413 cm−1 corresponds to the stretching vibration of hydroxyl groups, while other characteristic peaks are attributed to the BTC ligands.

The peaks at 3104 cm−1 and 2923 cm−1 are ascribed to the stretching vibration of C-H bonds in the aromatic ring structure of BTC ligands. The peaks at 1617 cm−1 and 1546 cm−1 are attributed to the skeleton vibration of the aromatic ring structure. The peak at 1105 cm−1 corresponds to the in-plane bending vibration of the aromatic ring structure.

Paired peaks are observed at 1440 cm−1, 1378 cm−1, 771 cm−1, and 715 cm−1, originating from the symmetric stretching vibration and variable angle vibration of carboxyl groups, respectively. The peak at 941 cm−1 is attributed to out-plane bending vibration of carboxyl groups. The FTIR spectra show some changes in vibration peaks after surface modification with PEI. Additional peaks associated with the stretching vibration of amino groups appear at 2948 cm−1 and 2842 cm−1. Peaks at 1523 cm−1 and 1386 cm−1 are assigned to the bending and stretching vibration of amide bonds, resulting from the covalent coupling of carboxyl and amino groups. Other peaks exhibit minimal changes after PEI modification, indicating that the surface modification has almost no effect on the framework structure of Ln-MOF. Furthermore, Fig. 1d illustrates the change in Zeta potential of Ln-MOF from −14.27 mV to 4.51 mV after PEI modification, primarily due to the conversion of surface carboxyl groups to amino groups. As shown in Fig. 1d, the Zeta potential of the Ln-MOF changes from −14.27 mV to 4.51 mV after the PEI modification. It is mainly owing to the conversion of surface carboxyl groups to amino groups. The Nitrogen adsorption–desorption isotherms of Ln-MOF and Ln-MOF@PEI are depicted in Fig. 1e and f. The calculated BET surface area for Ln-MOF and Ln-MOF@PEI are 320.825 and 21.603 m2/g, respectively. The average pore sizes are 5.319 and 5.351 nm for Ln-MOF and Ln-MOF@PEI. It implies that the PEI modification results in a reduction in the specific surface area of the Ln-MOF.

The emission spectra of the Ln-MOF are obtained under the 297 nm excitation. As shown in Fig. 2 , a series of Ln-MOF samples with different doping concentration of Tb3+ and Y3+ all exhibit a broad emission band centered at 402 nm and characteristic narrow bands of Tb3+ at 489 nm, 546 nm, 584 nm and 622 nm. The broad emission band at 402 nm belongs to the π − π* transition of BTC ligands. The characteristic emissions of Tb3+ at 489 nm, 546 nm, 584 nm and 622 nm are ascribed to the 5D4 → 7F6, 5D4 → 7F5, 5D4 → 7F4 and 5D4 → 7F3, respectively (Fig. 2b). Among the transitions, the relative strongest emission occurs in 546 nm. The rise of Tb3+ doping concentration is not always accompanied by the improvement of emission intensity. The maximum emission intensity was obtained at the 10 % of Tb3+ concentration. Further increasing the Tb3+ doping concentration resulted in concentration quenching, negatively affecting the luminescence performance of Ln-MOF@PEI. To achieve optimal detection sensitivity, the Ln-MOF (Tb3+ 0.10, Y3+ 0.90)@PEI was chosen as the probe for Cu2+ and Fe3+ detection.

The deionized water dispersion of Ln-MOF@PEI shows a pH-dependent luminescence behavior. Emission spectra of the dispersion were obtained at different pH values while maintaining a consistent concentration of Ln-MOF@PEI (Fig. 3 ). The emission intensity of Tb3+ and ligands initially increased and then decreased with changing pH values. The emission intensity reached its maximum at pH 8. The pH value of 8 was determined as the optimal parameter for Cu2+ and Fe3+ detection experiments. Additionally, the broad emission of ligands was enhanced in the deionized water dispersion. This enhancement is attributed to the effect of polar groups in the aqueous solution on the energy transfer among the ligands and Tb3+. The stability of Ln-MOFs was studied. The Ln-MOFs were dispersed in deionized water for 30 days. The emission spectra of the dispersion were recorded every 10 days (Fig. S1). The emission intensity at 402 nm and 546 nm of the dispersion decreased to 91.7 % and 95.2 % within 30 days, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Ratiometric luminescence detection of Cu2+ and Fe3+
ce:section.ce:para: A ratiometric luminescence detection was achieved for sensitive quantitative detection of Cu2+ and Fe3+ based on the Ln-MOF@PEI. Upon the addition of Cu2+ or Fe3+ solution, luminescence quenching of Ln-MOF@PEI occurred. The luminescence of ligands and Tb3+ showed the different quenching amplitude in presence of Cu2+ and Fe3+. Cu2+ induced a stronger quenching effect on the luminescence of Tb3+. It may stem from other quenching mechanisms. The absorption and emission spectrum of the Ln-MOF@PEI-Cu2+ complexes are shown in Fig. 4 a, demonstrating an overlap between the absorption band and the emission peaks. It indicates the contribution of inner filter effect to the luminescence quenching of Tb3+ [26–28]. The luminescence lifetime at 546 nm of Ln-MOF@PEI reduced from 1340.9 μs to 1238.4 μs in the presence of Cu2+ (Fig. 4b). It implies a reduction in radiative transition efficiency of Tb3+ [29–31]. These factors contribute to the differences in quenching amplitudes observed in the presence of Cu2+ and Fe3+. It enables Ln-MOF@PEI to distinguish and quantify Cu2+ and Fe3+ based on the luminescence intensity ratio of ligands and Tb3+. The XPS spectra were further performed to explore the chemical state and the surface characteristics of Ln-MOF, Ln-MOF@PEI and Ln-MOF@PEI-Cu2+ complex. As shown in Fig. S2a, the binding peaks of C 1 s, N 1 s, Y 3d, Tb 3d are observed in the XPS spectra of Ln-MOF, Ln-MOF@PEI and Ln-MOF@PEI-Cu2+, and the Cu 2p binding peaks are displayed in the Ln-MOF@PEI-Cu2+. The detailed C 1 s spectrum of Ln-MOF exhibits four symmetrical peaks at 284.8, 286.6, 288.7 and 290.8 eV in Fig. S2b. They are assigned to the C-C, C-O, C = O and O-C = O functional groups. It is noticed that the binding peaks at around 288.5 eV appearing in the Ln-MOF@PEI and Ln-MOF@PEI-Cu2+. They are originated from the C-N bond. The N 1 s peaks centered at around 399.4 eV are attributed to the amino groups of PEI (Fig. S2c). The Y 3d spectra of Ln-MOF, Ln-MOF@PEI and Ln-MOF@PEI-Cu2+ show no obvious differences (Fig. S2d). They are deconvoluted into two symmetrical peaks at 158.5 (Y 3d5/2) and 160.6 eV (Y 3d3/2). The binding peaks at 1242.5 and 1276.9 eV of Tb 3d in Ln-MOF are assigned to Tb 3d5/2 and Tb 3d3/2 (Fig. S2e). The two peaks shift slightly in Ln-MOF@PEI and Ln-MOF@PEI-Cu2+ complex, demonstrating that the combination of PEI and Cu2+ has an impact on the coordination of Tb and BTC. The Cu 2p spectrum of Ln-MOF@PEI-Cu2+ complex is exhibited in Fig. S2f. The doublet peaks centered at 932.7 and 952.4 eV belong to metallic Cu species, while binding peaks centered at 934.7 and 954.5 eV corresponded to 2p3/2 and 2p1/2 of bivalent Cu2+, respectively. The satellite peak with binding energy of 943.0 eV are assigned to electronic shake-up of Cu 2p. The XPS results further indicate that the complex of Cu2+ and PEI enhances the probability of non-radiative transitions during the emission process of Tb3+.

As shown in Fig. 5 a and 5c, the emission of the Ln-MOF@PEI is switched off in the presence of Cu2+ or Fe3+. The emission intensity of Tb3+ and BTC ligands both decreased with the rise of Cu2+ or Fe3+ concentration. The quenching constant (K SV) was calculated according to the Stern-Volmer equation:
I 0
/ I = 1 +
K
SV
C
where I 0 and I represent the emission intensity of the Ln-MOF@PEI dispersion before and after the addition of Cu2+ or Fe3+, respectively. [C] is the concentration of Cu2+ or Fe3+ and K SV is the quenching constant. Fig. S3 shows the emission intensity at 546 nm and 402 nm of Ln-MOF@PEI dispersion and Cu2+ or Fe3+ concentration. The results suggest an increase in the emission intensity for Cu2+ and Fe3+ concentration. The intensity ratios of the emission peaks at 546 nm and 402 nm (I546 nm/I402 nm) showed opposite trends with the rise of Cu2+ or Fe3+ concentration. It is due to the difference in luminescence quenching mechanisms. As shown in Fig. 5b, the I546 nm/I402 nm shows a negative correlation with Cu2+ concentration. The data are the averages of 5 parallel experiments, and the error bars are derived from the standard deviation of the averages. It is linearly fitted with the logarithm of the Cu2+ concentration in the range of 5 nM – 100 μM. The correlation coefficient of the regression equation is 0.999. The limit of detection (LOD) was 9.7 nM, which was calculated according to the linear relationship and 3S b/m criterion. The S b is the standard deviation of the blank sample and the m is the slope of the linearity. The I546 nm/I402 nm shows a positive correlation with Fe3+ concentration. It is linearly fitted with the logarithm of the Fe3+ concentration in the range of 50 nM – 500 μM. The LOD was 37.4 nM (calculating by 3S b/m criterion). The assay performance of our method is compared with some reported methods for Cu2+ or Fe3+ detection (Table 1 ). The sensitivity and detection range of our method are comparable or superior to the listed methods.

The Ln-MOF@PEI probes were collected by centrifugation after Cu2+ and Fe3+ detection. The PXRD patterns and emission spectra of probes were tested to verify the performance stability for recycling (Fig. S4). The diffraction peaks show no obvious changes after the detection and recollection by centrifugation and drying. It indicates that the Ln-MOF@PEI probes have stable structure. Fig. S4b is the emission spectra of the Ln-MOF@PEI probes and the Ln-MOF@PEI probes recollecting after Cu2+ or Fe3+ detection. The emission intensity of probes decreased after Cu2+ or Fe3+ detection. It may stem from the luminescence decline resulting from the ion adsorption and the formation of complexes between PEI and ions. The SEM images of Ln-MOF@PEI and Ln-MOF@PEI-Cu2+ complex are shown in Fig. S5. The morphology of Ln-MOF@PEI shows no significant changes after detection. These results indicate the stability of the Ln-MOF@PEI probe.

The selectivity study is used to evaluate the capability of the Ln-MOF@PEI probes for resisting the interference from other common anions and cations. All the selectivity experiments were carried out in the presence of high concentration interfering ions (1 mM). The response of the probes for different interfering ions was investigated. Fig. 6. records the emission intensity of Ln-MOF@PEI dispersion at 546 nm and 402 nm in the presence of specific ions. The error bars are originated from the standard deviation of the averages of 5 parallel experiments. The changes of emission intensity demonstrate that Ln-MOF@PEI has a selective response towards Cu2+ and Fe3+. The emission intensity has no tremendous decrease or increase after the addition of other common metal ions and anions. The results show that Ln-MOF@PEI probes have the acceptable selectivity for Cu2+ and Fe3+.

The practical application potential of Ln-MOF@PEI probes was verified by detecting Cu2+ and Fe3+ in real water samples. The Cu2+ and Fe3+ concentration of the real water sample is determined as 27.3 μM and 4.7 μM (average of 5 determinations, RSD = 1.31 % and 1.95 %) by atomic absorption spectrometry, respectively. The real water samples were detected by the Ln-MOF@PEI probes. The results were 26.9 μM (Cu2+ concentration, average of 5 determinations, RSD = 2.26 %) and 4.6 μM (Fe3+ concentration, average of 5 determinations, RSD = 1.53 %). It indicates that the detection performance of Ln-MOF@PEI probes is comparable to current commercial ion detection methods.

In summary, a ratio luminescence probe was developed for Cu2+ and Fe3+ detection based on Ln-MOF@PEI. Cu2+ and Fe3+ exhibit different quenching mechanisms on luminescence of Ln-MOF@PEI. The accurate quantitative detection of Cu2+ and Fe3+ was achieved by monitoring the changes in the intensity ratio of Tb3+ and ligands as a function of concentration. The LOD is determined as 9.7 nM for Cu2+ and 37.4 nM for Fe3+. The assay performance of the probe is comparable or superior to other methods reported in recent years. This work is expected to facilitate the practical application of ratio fluorescence detection technology.

Kexin Wang: Conceptualization, Investigation, Data curation, Validation, Formal analysis, Visualization, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Zhenglin Han: Investigation, Data curation, Formal analysis, Visualization, Methodology. Xiaokun Wen: Data curation, Formal analysis. Xiaoxuan Fan: Writing – review & editing, Formal analysis. Feifei Yin: Data curation, Funding acquisition. Baijie Guan: Supervision. Xia hong: Methodology, Funding acquisition. Jiwei Wang: Supervision, Methodology, Funding acquisition. Wen Liu: Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition. Tianya Tan: Supervision, Data curation, Validation, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grants No. 51972052), the Open Foundation of Key Laboratory for UV-Emitting Materials and Technology of Ministry of Education (Northeast Normal University, No. 135131009), the Doctoral Start-up Foundation and Natural Science Foundation of Liaoning Province (2021-BS-085 and 2021-MS-148), Shenyang Science and Technology Bureau (22-315-6-06) and General Project of the Department of Education of Liaoning Province (LJKZ0084).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2023.109668.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Cerebral bioimaging of Cu, Fe, Zn, and Mn in the MPTP mouse model of Parkinson's disease using laser ablation inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (LA-ICP-MS)

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A. Matusch, C. Depboylu, C. Palm, B. Wu, G.U. Hoeglinger, M.K.H. Schaefer, J.S.
